title: Data in rows and columns
why.no-table: You are still lining things up with spaces. Run it and look — the columns are gone. The browser swallows spaces, you know this from lesson 18.
why.no-tr: There is a wrapper, but no rows. Each row gets wrapped in <tr>.
why.no-td: There are rows, but no cells. Each thing in the row gets wrapped in <td>.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. In tables it is easy to lose a .
blocks.0.text: You are selling two bikes and you want to compare them — model, price, wheel size.
blocks.1.text: You lined them up with spaces, like on a typewriter. Run it.
blocks.2.text: They scattered. The columns are gone. The browser swallows your spaces — you know this from lesson 18. There is no fooling it here.
blocks.5.text: <table> is the table itself. <tr> is one row — from "table row". <td> is one cell — from "table data".
blocks.6.text: You write row by row, left to right. Exactly as you read.
blocks.8.text: Notice what is missing: there is no tag for a column. There is no <tc>.
blocks.9.text: Columns happen by themselves. If every row has three cells — there are three columns. If one row has four and the others three — the table breaks and you see it immediately.
blocks.10.text: You write rows. Columns are a consequence.
blocks.12.text: Run it and look. No lines, no borders, no separators. The cells are glued together and barely readable.
blocks.14.text: The table is for the data. The lines are for the eyes. And eyes are a job for CSS.
blocks.15.text: Every beautiful table you have ever seen is exactly this underneath: <tr> and <td>. Nothing more.
blocks.17.text: Turn the three paragraphs into a real table. Three rows, three columns.
blocks.18.text: Run it and compare: the spaces vanished, the columns stayed.
blocks.19.text: But something is off. The first row — "Model, Price, Wheels" — is not data. It is the names of the columns.
blocks.20.text: And you told it <td>. Meaning: "just another data cell". The next lesson is about that.
